title: Dog Vomiting Green Liquid: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog vomiting green liquid? Learn why bile causes this color, if it's an emergency, and how to triage your pet's symptoms effectively.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green liquid vomit in dogs is often bile and typically indicates an empty stomach, but it requires veterinary attention if it occurs multiple times or is accompanied by lethargy.

Why is my dog vomiting green liquid?

When a dog vomits green liquid, it is most often bile that has been dyed by stomach contents or has sat in the small intestine. Bile is a digestive fluid produced by the liver, and while it is naturally yellow, it can appear green if it mixes with grass, specific dyes in food, or if it is expelled quickly from the upper digestive tract. It usually indicates that the stomach is empty, but the underlying reason for the vomiting needs to be addressed.

Is it an emergency if my dog vomits green liquid?

  • Minor Upset: If your dog vomits once but remains energetic and continues to eat and drink, the situation is likely not an immediate emergency.
  • Medium Urgency: If the vomiting occurs multiple times, is accompanied by lethargy, or if your dog cannot keep water down, the urgency level is classified as Medium.
  • Veterinary Action: You should contact your veterinarian or an emergency clinic within a few hours to prevent dehydration and further complications.

Why should I take a photo of the vomit for my veterinarian?

  • Visual Documentation: Take a clear photo of the vomit before cleaning it up so the vet can see the exact shade and consistency, such as whether it is watery or foamy.
  • Identifying Triggers: A photo helps the vet identify traces of blood, grass, or foreign objects that may be causing the distress.
  • Diagnostic Triage: These visual cues provide vital information that helps the veterinary team determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, vomiting green liquid (bile) can be attributed to various causes, including dietary indiscretion or intolerance (ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods, or foreign material), drug or toxin ingestion (antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illness (pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, hypoadrenocorticism), endoparasitism (Physaloptera sp), or bacterial (Helicobacter-associated disease) or viral (canine parvovirus gastroenteritis) infection. Diagnosis is based on history, clinical findings, and response to treatment; further diagnostics are warranted if foreign objects or toxins are suspected, signs persist beyond 2 days of symptomatic therapy, hematemesis or melena are present, the animal is systemically unwell, or abnormalities are noted on abdominal palpation. Initial treatment for acute vomiting includes discontinuing oral intake for 24 hours, followed by small, frequent feedings of a bland, low-fat diet, with a gradual return to the usual diet over 3-5 days. Antiemetics such as metoclopramide (0.3 mg/kg, PO or SC, tid, or 1-2 mg/kg/day CRI), maropitant (1 mg/kg/day, SC, or 2 mg/kg/day, PO, for 5 days), and ondansetron (0.1-1 mg/kg, PO, once to twice daily) may be considered after an etiologic diagnosis or in cases of protracted vomiting causing dehydration or electrolyte imbalances.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: Can eating grass make my dog's vomit green?
faqs.answer: Yes, grass contains chlorophyll which can stain stomach fluids a bright green color if the dog vomits shortly after ingestion.

faqs.question: What should I do if my dog is vomiting green and acting tired?
faqs.answer: If vomiting is paired with lethargy, it is a sign of a more serious systemic issue. You should contact a veterinarian immediately for an evaluation.

faqs.question: Should I fast my dog after they vomit green liquid?
faqs.answer: While a 12-hour fast can sometimes settle the stomach, you should only do this under the guidance of a vet to ensure your dog doesn't become dehydrated or hypoglycemic.
